/22.title: What I wish I knew at 22 and so might you!
/22.summary: Igor's intern-program talk "What I wish I knew at 22," distilling advice on living a good life and optimizing a software-engineering career, with links to its sections and a referenced thread arguing you should optimize for fun rather than future reward.
entities: What I wish I knew at 22

/40yo.title: Age Before Beauty: The Vanishing Act of Older Coders
/40yo.summary: Igor's half-baked hypotheses on where programmers go as they age out of the industry, weighing whether age discrimination is real, cataloging age-based cognitive declines versus experience-based advantages, and arguing AI tools could level the playing field for older developers.

/42.title: What I wish I knew at 42
/42.summary: Igor's life-advice talk on what he wishes he knew at 42, covering how to navigate your 40s: managing declining health like a financial portfolio, getting preventive screenings (colonoscopy, vision, hearing), and weighing a gap year at 50 versus grinding to traditional retirement. It blends practical medical guidance with reflections on wisdom, balance, and what matters as you age.

/4dx.title: The 4 Disciplines of Execution (4DX)
/4dx.summary: A structured summary of the 4 Disciplines of Execution (4DX) framework for executing strategic priorities amid daily operational demands, covering focusing on wildly important goals, lead vs. lag measures, scoreboards, and accountability cadence. It connects 4DX to related ideas from Essentialism and the 7 Habits.

/7-habits.title: Seven Habits: A manual for life
/7-habits.summary: A personal reference to Stephen Covey's "The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People," which Igor uses as the foundation for his self-improvement and coaching, organized around the dependent-to-independent-to-interdependent progression with reflection prompts for each habit.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
The 7 Habits of Highly Effective Teens
Begin with the End in Mind
Seek First to Understand Then to be Understood

/7h-concepts.title: 7 habits concepts
/7h-concepts.summary: Igor's distilled notes on the foundational concepts that precede Stephen Covey's 7 Habits — paradigms, principles, the maturity continuum, P/PC balance, and inside-out change — woven together with mental-model ideas like map-vs-terrain and reframing. It frames character over personality, borrowed strength as a failure mode, and effectiveness as balancing results against the capacity that produces them.

/90days.title: The first 90 days
/90days.summary: A structured summary and study notes of Michael Watkins' book "The First 90 Days," a how-to manual for leaders starting a new job, with Igor's personal reflections on his own transitions joining Amazon and Facebook/Meta.

/Canadian-Spotting.title: Canadian Spotting
/Canadian-Spotting.summary: A short storytelling post where Igor wins a bar bet about spotting a Canadian by recounting the 1907 Quebec Bridge collapse that killed Mohawk steel workers, and the engineering Iron Ring tradition born from it as a reminder of ethical obligation.

/Immutable-Laws-Of-Hard.title: The Immutable Laws of Hard Things
/Immutable-Laws-Of-Hard.summary: A short list of "immutable laws" that govern hard self-improvement efforts (exercise, diet, personal development): you need a measurable goal or you drift away from it, you'll only barely hit goals rather than beat them, and without consequences or rewards you'll ignore the goal entirely.

/The-Genesis-Node.title: Microsoft Azure - The Genesis Node
/The-Genesis-Node.summary: A first-person workplace anecdote from Igor's time on Microsoft Azure's newly formed DevOps team: a panic over a stolen "genesis node" (a single point of failure that spawns and heals all fabrics) turns out to be the OS team innocently grabbing it for destructive testing. It closes with lessons about Hanlon's razor, labeling critical infrastructure, and asking follow-up questions.

/act.title: ACT: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, or Reverse-Engineered Buddhism
/act.summary: Notes on Applied Buddhism, the convergence of ACT therapy, Buddhist equanimity, and the 7 Habits stimulus-response gap. Pain is the signal, suffering is what we add, equanimity is the skill of not adding it.

/activation.title: Activation Energy
/activation.summary: A mental model for willpower and habit formation built on "activation energy" — the energy needed to stop a current activity and start a new one. It frames willpower as a distributed system of role-based "muscle fibers" (professional, physical, emotional, family, creative) that can compound into death spirals or be supercharged by morning habits, schedules, and good sleep.

/adblocker-trojan.title: The Mystery of the google ads
/adblocker-trojan.summary: A first-person debugging walkthrough of discovering, diagnosing, and removing a trojan that injected ads into Google search results via a compromised Chrome extension, likely installed through a brand-jacked NPM package. Covers the detection process (iframe inspection, network tab analysis) and the suspected root cause around malware like Adrozek that modifies browser extensions.

/addiction.title: Escape Artists: The True Face of Addiction
/addiction.summary: Igor's personal framework for distinguishing addiction (compelled + life worsened) from passion (compelled + net positive) and hobby (not compelled), with practical in-the-moment tests and a catalog of his own current, hypo, and beaten addictions like TikTok, social-media analytics, work/success addiction, and alcohol.

/advertising.title: Advertising
/advertising.summary: A personal reference glossary explaining how the advertising industry works, covering why brands advertise, how much companies spend, and the key terminology and ad products of digital and TV advertising.

/advice.title: The Advice Lab: Where Wisdom Gets Beta Tested
/advice.summary: A playful, software-metaphor-framed guide to giving and receiving life advice — covering the two universal limits of advice (no advice fits everyone or everyone always), a "giving protocol" rooted in seeking to understand first, and a "receiving protocol" of running incoming advice through skeptical evaluation.

/affirmations.title: Affirmations
/affirmations.summary: Igor's personal daily affirmations practice: the four affirmations he writes out each day (Do It Anyways/discipline, Essentialism, Class Act/compassion, Calm Like Water/presence), their evolution over time, and a gamified three-level system for actually applying and reviewing them.
entities: begin with the end in mind

/agency.title: Agency trumps Intelligence
/agency.summary: In the AI era, agency (bias for action, shipping and iterating) matters more than raw intelligence or accumulated technical knowledge, because AI commoditizes technical execution and pushes the threshold for needing a deep technologist later in a product's lifecycle. Includes practical habits for high-IQ people stuck in analysis paralysis.

/ai.title: All posts on AI
/ai.summary: A landing/hub page for Igor's AI writing, anchored by an essay framing AI as the eighth major technology revolution that makes expertise and decision-making cheap (contrasted with computing making logic cheap), plus scattered notes on how LLMs think, training inefficiency, prompt "alchemy," and where order-of-magnitude AI improvements will come from.

/ai-art.title: AI and art
/ai-art.summary: An exploratory post on how AI can augment creativity and art, centered on the "sandwich" workflow (human prompts, AI generates options, human edits) and a survey of generative AI tools across images, text-to-speech, music, and video.

/ai-bestie.title: Igor's AI Bestie Simulator
/ai-bestie.summary: Igor documents an experiment building an AI simulation of his best friend by fine-tuning GPT-3.5 on years of chat history, then giving it embodiment via voice cloning and a Discord/voice-call interface. It covers the data-prep, fine-tuning, and tooling lessons learned along the way.

/ai-bm.title: AI Business Models
/ai-bm.summary: An analysis of how AI reshapes software business models — commoditizing anything fully specifiable (docs, component libraries, templates) and breaking search-based discovery funnels, using Tailwind Labs' collapse as the case study, while operations and human-optimized content survive. Igor then applies this lens to his own coaching and blog.

/ai-cockpit.title: The AI Cockpit: My Control Surface for Multi-Agent Development
/ai-cockpit.summary: Igor describes his "cockpit" — a stack of physical and software tools (voice input, a Rust tmux picker, Alfred, an agent dashboard, Stream Deck, Tailscale) that gives him visibility into and instant switching between multiple slow AI coding agents running in parallel. The transferable skill is building a human interface for AI delegation: visibility, fast switching, and ambient awareness when orchestrating autonomous agents.

/ai-developer.title: AI Developer
/ai-developer.summary: A framework and field guide for "AI developers" (AI application engineers) who build products on top of AI models, covering the mindset shift from coding-with-AI to product-thinking, "10 commandments" of AI development, levels of cognitive architecture/autonomy, design patterns, model selection, and a strategic map of the AI product landscape.

/ai-faq.title: AI FAQ
/ai-faq.summary: A FAQ-style post where Igor collects recurring questions he gets about AI and shares his current, deliberately unresolved takes — covering whether AI thinks, AI's effect on human thinking and relationships, who controls AI, bias and copyright, and accelerationist vs doomer futures.

/ai-feed.title: AI Feed — Curated Reading Through Claude
/ai-feed.summary: Igor's curated AI reading feed, processed via "Randy the Raccoon" (an AI reading partner that fetches, summarizes, and predicts interest), capturing links on AI-native engineering, management, and craft along with his debrief reactions. It doubles as recommender training data documenting the content attributes and creators he gravitates toward.

/ai-hiring.title: AI Hiring: How Should Hiring Change in an AI World?
/ai-hiring.summary: Igor's evolving notes on how hiring and technical interviews are changing in the AI era — covering what companies are doing about AI use in interviews, the cheating arms race, and his argument that AI raises the value of durable traits (agency, relentlessness, curiosity, heart, EQ) over raw technical knowledge.
entities: Smart Gets Things Done Not an Asshole

/ai-image.title: AI Image Generation
/ai-image.summary:
A hands-on, work-in-progress guide to generating AI images of yourself using the Flux image model with personal LoRAs, including blending multiple LoRAs (e.g., putting yourself into a Mona Lisa scene) and making talking-head videos. Covers the basics of how diffusion and LoRAs work and how to run them on Replicate.</parameter>
</invoke>

/ai-journal.title: Igor's AI Journal
/ai-journal.summary: An ongoing AI journal where Igor logs experiments and lessons from working with AI coding agents: how AI widens his option space for creative picks, picking the right problem before optimizing cost, Dylan Patel on the economics of AI token scarcity, least-privilege GitHub tokens for CI archiving, agent-to-agent code review, and the runaway cost of heavy Claude Code usage.

/ai-native-manager.title: The AI Native Engineering Manager
/ai-native-manager.summary: An engineering manager's working notes on what it means to lead a software team in the AI-native era — reframing chaos as opportunity, coaching people through the stages of AI adoption (denial to sustainable execution), and rethinking team roles, careers, and the EM job itself.

/ai-operator.title: The AI Operator: Learning to Drive the Machine
/ai-operator.summary: A guide to operating AI agents as a learnable skill covering thinking tokens on the loop supervision review gates voice throwing away bad output and parallel agents

/ai-optimism.title: What I love about AI
/ai-optimism.summary: An optimism essay arguing AI is a net positive force: it personalizes learning, lowers the cost of creating, and collapses the "entry tax" on starting small ventures (like Sergio's micro-school), producing more cultural diversity rather than homogenization. It rebuts the doomer narrative on jobs, homogenization, and AI slop.

/ai-paper.title: Seminal AI Papers
/ai-paper.summary: A curated reading list of AI papers and essay series aimed at practitioners (not just researchers), covering LLM agent architecture (planning, memory, tool use) and Leopold Aschenbrenner's "Situational Awareness" series on the path from AGI to superintelligence and its geopolitical/security stakes.

/ai-relationships.title: AI Relationships - When the Chatbot Is Better at Caring Than Your Friends
/ai-relationships.summary: An essay arguing that AI chatbots are already preferred over humans for emotional support, and that outsourcing care to them atrophies our capacity for vulnerability, connection, and shared human infrastructure. It proposes concrete countermeasures — using AI as triage not treatment, protecting a "vulnerability budget," demanding friction/disagreement from companion AIs, and preserving human-only spaces.

/ai-second-brain.title: AI as Your Second Brain
/ai-second-brain.summary: Argues that AI removes the human-librarian bottleneck that doomed traditional second-brain/PKM systems, and lays out how to build an AI second brain via a flywheel of available context, pre-briefing before work, and debriefing after — feeding personas like Larry, Tony, and Randy that reason over your accumulated personal data.

/ai-security.title: AI Security
/ai-security.summary: A personal reference notebook on the intersection of AI and security, cataloging LLM attack vectors (prompt injection, jailbreaking, exfiltration, visual prompt injection, flow jacking, bijection learning), the OWASP Top 10 for LLMs, and AI safety/ethics topics like user wellbeing protections and who decides what AI should say.

/ai-testing.title: Testing AI
/ai-testing.summary: A technical guide to testing and evaluating LLM/AI outputs, covering eval systems, LLM-as-judge, blind taste tests, and tooling for comparing models and prompts. It walks through concepts (tracing, A/B testing, subjective judging) and concrete examples like rating which model is funnier or a better git-commit summarizer.

/ai-training.title: Training LLMs
/ai-training.summary: A concise notes/reference post cataloging large language model training and fine-tuning techniques, covering concepts like LoRA, RAG grounding, quantization, RLHF/RLAIF/DPO preference optimization, and model file formats. It reads as Igor's personal cheat-sheet of LLM training jargon rather than a deep tutorial.

/amazon.title: My thoughts on Amazon
/amazon.summary: A reflective retrospective on the author's four years at Amazon, cataloging the good (frugality, writing culture, operational excellence, leadership principles, working backwards) and the bad (Day 2 culture, hyper-growth scaling problems, a broken promo process), plus personal management lessons learned.

/anger.title: Anger is the enemy
/anger.summary: A personal essay framing anger as a destructive intoxicant that impairs judgment, opening with the author flipping off his wife after a chaotic school-morning miscommunication. It breaks anger into components (mental pain plus a feeling of being trapped) and offers graduated antidotes along the frustration-to-rage intensity dial: tolerance, compassion, and hope.

/anxiety.title: Anxiety, the gap between reality and expectations
/anxiety.summary: A personal framework for understanding and managing anxiety, defining it as the gap between reality and the desire to control, and offering antidotes like shifting from control to influence, widening perspective across time, and distinguishing how much you "care" from how much something objectively "matters."
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

/anxiety-management.title: Anxiety Management
/anxiety-management.summary: A personal protocol for managing acute anxiety: first rebuild realistic perspective (label the emotion, identify the precise perceived threat, estimate probabilities, calibrate self-confidence), then shift focus from controlling to influencing the situation. It draws on cognitive reframing, exposure over avoidance, meditation, and box breathing.

/appreciate.title: Appreciate
/appreciate.summary: A short, unfinished stub post collecting Igor's early thoughts on appreciation as a practice — how being appreciated feels, appreciating people versus circumstances, its relationship to gratitude, and the open question of whether appreciating people lets them settle for less.

/ar.title: Augmented Reality
/ar.summary: Igor's personal notes and glossary on augmented and virtual reality, cataloging core concepts (SLAM, VIO, IMU, AR Cloud), hardware, monetization, use cases, and philosophical questions about why physical reality beats virtual.

/asciinema.title: asciinema
/asciinema.summary: A technical scratch/test page experimenting with how to embed asciinema terminal-session recordings into the Jekyll blog, comparing the custom include helper against raw asciinema-player and script-tag embed syntaxes. It contains no real prose — just placeholder embeds and notes-to-self.

/ast-grep.title: AST Grep
/ast-grep.summary: A short technical walkthrough of using ast-grep (invoked as `sg`) to search and transform code via abstract syntax trees, demonstrated by extracting LLM-consumable documentation from the Python-generated docs of the hyper-div library using pattern matching and YAML rule configs.

/awareness.title: Awareness: The Perils and Opportunities of Reality
/awareness.summary: A chapter-by-chapter summary of Anthony de Mello's book "Awareness," which argues that most people live unconsciously ("asleep") and that non-judgmental self-observation -- distinguishing the observing "I" from the labeled "me" -- is the path to happiness, freedom, and detachment. Includes a computing analogy for engineers and ties the book's awareness meditation to Google's Search Inside Yourself program.
entities: Awareness: The Perils and Opportunities of Reality

/back-pain.title: Back Pain: Stuart McGill and the Big 3
/back-pain.summary: A practical overview of Stuart McGill's approach to back pain: the spine should stay stiff while motion comes from the hips and shoulders, with the McGill Big 3 isometric exercises (curl-up, side plank, bird dog) building muscular endurance and stability to prevent injury.

/balance.title: Balance - The Holy Grail
/balance.summary: A draft outline exploring how to achieve balance across the many roles in life by treating energy as a finite resource, setting realistic per-role goals, and monitoring actual-vs-desired investment. It pulls in productivity frameworks (Parkinson's law, 80/20, peak-end rule) to argue success should be measured by impact, not energy spent.
entities: Begin with the end in mind

/balloon.title: Being a Ballooner
/balloon.summary: Igor's account of taking up balloon-twisting in 2022 as a low-effort, high-impact way to deliver joy to kids (and parents), with practical notes on inflators, technique, and the psychology of why balloons delight people.

/bc.title: BC Vacation Guide: Vancouver & Chilliwack Adventures
/bc.summary: A personal travel guide to favorite spots in British Columbia, Canada — covering Vancouver, Victoria, and Chilliwack — with recommendations for attractions, food, suspension bridges, waterfalls, hotels, and practical travel tips like border crossings and getting around.

/be-proactive.title: Be Proactive
/be-proactive.summary: A personal distillation of Stephen Covey's first habit, "Be Proactive," reframing it around emotional self-regulation, agency, and the discipline of choosing your response between stimulus and response. Covers equanimity, proactive language, the Circle of Influence vs. Circle of Concern, and keeping commitments to yourself.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

/being-a-great-manager.title: Being the best manager I can be
/being-a-great-manager.summary: Igor's collected research and personal philosophy on being a great engineering manager, covering how he measures success (team culture, results, cross-team partnership) and the concrete behaviors he practices: coaching, recognition, clear communication, hiring, and establishing team process.

/bike.title: Biking
/bike.summary: Igor's personal notes on biking: his folding and e-cargo/everyday bike collection, recommended accessories, and an explainer on e-bike mechanics (mid vs rear drive, throttles vs pedal assist, torque/power/watts).

/bike-tesla-identity.title: How My Tesla's AI Life Coach Got Me Back on My Bike
/bike-tesla-identity.summary: Igor describes building "Tony," an AI life coach with a Tony Soprano persona that lives in his Tesla and is trained on his eulogy and values, then walks through a real conversation where Tony helps him reconcile his "car-free spirit" identity with owning a Tesla. The post frames this as a "Reflection Coach" — an AI trained on your values that mirrors your own words back to help you act in alignment with who you want to be.

/blueprint.title: Bryan Johnson - A 50 year old 18 year old
/blueprint.summary: Igor's reflections on Bryan Johnson's Blueprint longevity protocol, using it as a lens to think about sleep, discipline, self-accountability, and the idea of two competing selves (disciplined "morning Igor" vs impulsive "evening Igor") and whether to delegate decision-making to an algorithm.

/bmy.title: Business Model You
/bmy.summary: A worked example of applying the Business Model Canvas — a tool for mapping how a company makes money — to one's own career, breaking down personal key activities, resources, partners, customer segments (engineering team, product manager, boss, mentees), and a personal balance sheet of tangible and intangible revenue versus costs.

/books-that-defined-me.title: Books that defined me
/books-that-defined-me.summary: A curated, annotated list of books that shaped Igor's thinking, organized by theme — productivity and habits, emotional intelligence, pursuing your dreams, strategy, entrepreneurship, and creative thinking — with a one-line takeaway for each.
entities: Seven Habits of Highly Effective People

/breath.title: Breathing as if your life depends on it
/breath.summary: A comprehensive reference guide to breathing techniques (Box Breathing, Bellows Breath, Alternate Nostril, Psychological Sigh, Resonant Breathing, and more), explaining how inhale/exhale ratios and breath holds shift oxygen, CO2, and the nervous system to produce energized or calm states. It pairs step-by-step instructions and yoga names with the physiological science behind each pattern.

/brompton-toys.title: My Brompton and its accoutrements
/brompton-toys.summary: A practical roundup of accessories Igor bought for his Brompton folding bike, with recommendations on what's worth getting (EZ wheels, ergonomic grips, front bag, rack, folding lock, child seat) and why.

/browsers-for-machines.title: Browsers for Machines: The Headless Browser Landscape for AI Agents
/browsers-for-machines.summary: A hands-on comparison of three browser-automation approaches for AI coding agents — Lightpanda, Playwright CLI, and Playwright MCP — covering their architecture, token costs, latency, and failure modes, with guidance on when to use each (plus WebFetch and curl).

/brython.title: A playground for brython and pystory
/brython.summary: A technical playground page demonstrating Brython — Python running in the browser via WASM — including DOM manipulation examples and showing the loader script and Python include code.

/bucket-list.title: The Reverse Bucket List: Cross Out What You Don't Actually Want
/bucket-list.summary: A reflection on Arthur Brooks' "reverse bucket list" method—deliberately crossing out ego-driven desires to want less rather than achieve more—and Igor applies it to his own life, keeping family- and presence-oriented goals while discarding status-driven ones.

/build-bubble-bike.title: Building a bubble bike
/build-bubble-bike.summary: Igor documents building a "bubble bike" — a bicycle rigged with a custom bubble machine to spread joy to others — and walks through the iterative hardware problems he solved (mounting, power, bubble-juice storage/leaks, frothing, melting plastic).

/build-life-you-want.title: Build The Life YOU Want
/build-life-you-want.summary: A chapter-by-chapter summary of Arthur Brooks and Oprah Winfrey's book "Build the Life You Want," distilling its science-backed framework for happiness: managing emotions through metacognition and substitution, and investing in the four pillars of family, friendship, work, and faith. It covers Brooks' happiness equations, the reverse bucket list, and the idea that the most valuable friendships are "useless" ones pursued for their own sake.

/cache.title: Shipping incubations: Cache
/cache.summary: Igor recounts joining the Microsoft Garage team to ship Cache (the cross-platform clipboard/clipping app that evolved from the OneClip incubation), taking a product from internal incubation to market across Windows and iOS. It covers the work of recruiting talent, closing gaps, and tackling product-market fit and logistics.

/caring.title: Caring - The story of emotional intensity
/caring.summary: An exploration of "caring" defined as the intensity of one's emotional response, arguing that caring is always an asset even when it causes suffering, and that habitually withholding caring to avoid pain can erode passion and contribute to depression. Draws on Dr. Raph's psychology of passion and Stoic philosophy to distinguish healthy caring (commitment, curiosity) from harmful caring (attachment, rumination, anxiety over things outside our control).
entities: circle of concern vs circle of influence

/cert-error.title: The Case of the Cert Validation Error
/cert-error.summary: A parent walks through debugging his 10-year-old son's website that went insecure when its TLS certificate expired, explained twice: once as a kid-friendly analogy (phone numbers, phone books, and the police) and once technically. The root cause was a misconfigured DNS A record that stopped GitHub Pages from reissuing the certificate.

/changelog.title: Changelog
/changelog.summary: A weekly changelog summarizing updates across Igor's blog and GitHub projects, covering a major augmentation of the 7 Habits chapter series, AI journal entries on token economics and the "wrong jungle" problem, and cross-repo work on AI agent orchestration (Gas City), iOS apps, and image-generation tooling.

/chapters.title: The chapters of our lives
/chapters.summary: A reflective essay framing life as a series of chapters and developmental stages, mapping what you have, learn, and should do across decades (20s through 60s), with advice on raising children and a survey of cross-cultural models for life's phases.

/chasing-daylight.title: Chasing Daylight: Imagine you're gonna die in less than 100 days
/chasing-daylight.summary: A reflection on Eugene O'Kelly's memoir "Chasing Daylight," in which the KPMG CEO, given three months to live after a terminal brain cancer diagnosis, applies his strategic mindset to dying consciously—accepting reality, creating "perfect moments" of presence, and "unwinding" his relationships with intentional goodbyes. Igor distills its lessons on mortality into his own framework for the pursuit of happiness.

/chop.title: The CHOP Shop: Where AI Meets Code
/chop.summary: An overview of CHOP (Chat-Oriented Programming) — a workflow where developers direct AI through high-level natural-language dialogue rather than hand-writing code. It surveys the evolution of AI coding, key tools (Cursor, Aider, Claude Code), practical tips/workflows, and essays on how AI is reshaping the developer role, junior-vs-senior dynamics, and the joy of coding.

/chow.title: CHOW: Chat-Oriented Writing
/chow.summary: CHOW Chat-Oriented Writing is Igor's framework for using AI to think better rather than write faster, treating the written artifact as a side effect of clarity in your own head.

/class-act.title: Be a class act
/class-act.summary: A note-form post outlining the practices of being a "class act" — seeking to understand, showing compassion, appreciating others, and not getting baited into petty conflict — illustrated with examples of gracious public figures and leaders.

/claw.title: Claws: The Next Layer of AI
/claw.summary: An explainer on "claws" — persistent, autonomous AI entities (a new layer atop LLM agents) that run on personal hardware, remember context about you, and communicate via messaging apps like WhatsApp. It traces the term's origin in OpenClaw, Karpathy's layered "onion" framing, the chaotic naming saga, and the serious security risks (Willison's "lethal trifecta") illustrated by real-world incidents.

/coaching.title: Coaching - How to help others find insight
/coaching.summary: A personal field guide to coaching others, framed as "midwifery for insight" — drawing out solutions from the coachee rather than telling them. It collects mindsets, go-to questions, situational-leadership frameworks, feedback techniques, and human-safety models (SCARF/TERA) for in-the-moment and long-term coaching.
entities: Self Leadership and the One Minute Manager

/coe.title: Learning from mistakes
/coe.summary: Amazon Correction of Errors COE process guide for fixing mistakes

/comp.title: Care about Total Compensation
/comp.summary: An explainer on how big-tech total compensation works: why you should compare total comp (salary + cash bonus + equity) rather than salary, how vesting schedules and company-specific grant models work, and concepts like the four-year cliff, signing-bonus math, and pay bands.

/content-audience.title: Content Audience
/content-audience.summary: Igor's personal content strategy document defining the purpose and audience for the videos and blog content he creates, organized into four channels (life documentary, emotional/life wisdom, AI experiments, and engineering management), plus guardrails for what to share. The core thesis is that feedback beats planning—start producing content to learn who it's for.
entities: Begin with the End in Mind

/content-creation.title: Content Creation: From Shaky Selfies to Social Superstar
/content-creation.summary: A practical how-to guide for solo video content creators, walking through the full production pipeline—gear (phone, mics, gimbals, 360 cameras), software (CapCut, teleprompter apps), scripting, editing, and the harsh realities of creator monetization.

/covid.title: Notes from Covid 19
/covid.summary: A personal reflection on staying productive and positive during the early COVID-19 pandemic, framed around the circle of concern vs the circle of influence: avoid mind monsters and crisis-porn news, focus on what you can act on, and look for the best in people during a crisis.

/curious.title: Isn't that curious
/curious.summary: A personal framework for replacing self-judgment and judgment of others with curiosity and compassion, using the "what would grandma do?" lens, a past/present/future model of compassion, and a structured reflection practice for handling broken rules and mistakes.

/d/2016-02-03-positive-computing-survey.title: Positive computing a whirlwind tour
/d/2016-02-03-positive-computing-survey.summary: A survey of "positive computing" software — apps and tools aimed at improving human well-being, organized by category: CBT activity apps, mental-health chatbots, mood trackers, meditation/mindfulness trainers, and wellness wearables. A brain-dump of the author's early thoughts on the emerging field and the products he has tried.

/d/2016-05-09-lost-interview.title: Steve Jobs -- The Lost Interview
/d/2016-05-09-lost-interview.summary: Unedited notes from Steve Jobs's "lost interview" (1995) covering his philosophy on product development, why product-focused cultures decay when sales and marketing take over, the value of harsh-but-respectful feedback to great people, and his predictions about the web as a communication and distribution channel.
entities: Good Artists Copy, Great Artists Steal

/d/2016-2-14-Health-In-The-Head.title: Emotional Health In My Head
/d/2016-2-14-Health-In-The-Head.summary: Igor draws a parallel between physical and emotional health: having the building blocks (running 5 miles, self-awareness) isn't enough without the discipline that completes them (dieting for the body, empathy and acceptance for emotions). What feels healthy "in your head" isn't the same as being healthy in reality.

/d/2016-2-15-Seth-Godin-Notes.title: Seth Godin- Ideas
/d/2016-2-15-Seth-Godin-Notes.summary: Notes distilling Seth Godin's core ideas on thriving as a creator in the modern economy: choosing yourself over legacy distribution, building a tribe rather than being a replaceable cog, fostering connection over perfection, and using "The Dip" framework to decide when to quit versus lean in.

/d/2016-7-14-Customer-Service.title: Companies with customer obsession
/d/2016-7-14-Customer-Service.summary: A short business-strategy reflection arguing that customer obsession (expressed through great customer service) is a key competitive differentiator that drives retention and free word-of-mouth advertising, and sketching how it reshapes a company's business model (key resources, activities, costs, and revenue).

/d/2016-8-1-Twelve-Tech-Forces.title: 12 Tech forces that will shape our future
/d/2016-8-1-Twelve-Tech-Forces.summary: Personal notes summarizing Kevin Kelly's book "The Inevitable" and its twelve technological forces shaping the digital future, with emphasis on the shift from ownership to streaming/flowing, how to add value when copies are free, and the evolution of reading from word to book to screen.

/d/2017-07-22-vacationphotos.title: Vacation Photos
/d/2017-07-22-vacationphotos.summary: Igor shares practical tips for taking vacation and "reminiscing" photos, reviewing the gear he travels with: the iPhone 7 camera, Google Maps Timelines, selfie sticks, and 360-degree cameras.

/d/2017-09-10-software-rot.title: Software Rot
/d/2017-09-10-software-rot.summary: A short narrative reflection on software rot: returning to a hobby project after a year, the author finds the toolchain badly outdated and the dependency upgrades painful despite having tests and automated builds, only to discover the change he wanted to make had already been made a year earlier.

/d/2017-11-04-clean-architecture.title: Clean Architecture
/d/2017-11-04-clean-architecture.summary: Igor's book-notes outline of Robert C. Martin's "Clean Architecture," summarizing its chapters on object-oriented design (the SOLID principles), component cohesion and coupling principles, and software architecture principles like boundaries, the dependency rule, and treating databases/web/frameworks as details.

/d/2018-02-10-you-do-want-your-favorite-.title: Software as a service is good for users
/d/2018-02-10-you-do-want-your-favorite-.summary: An economic argument for why you should want your favorite app to use subscription pricing: subscriptions force publishers to keep proving value and reinvesting, whereas one-time-purchase apps (constrained by app-store economics that block paid upgrades) incentivize developers to stop investing in an app once its user base is saturated and move on to a new one.

/d/2018-09-23-mind-lines.title: Mind-Lines
/d/2018-09-23-mind-lines.summary: A bare numbered list of "Mind Lines" — the conversational reframing/sleight-of-mouth patterns from NLP (e.g. specificity, outcome framing, counter-example framing, identity framing) used to shift how a listener frames a belief or statement.

/d/apollo.title: Quality
/d/apollo.summary: A collection of two foundational Apollo-program texts — JFK's "We choose to go to the Moon" speech and Gene Kranz's "Tough and Competent" address to Mission Control after the Apollo 1 fire — curated as inspiration on ambition, accountability, and quality.
entities: We choose to go to the Moon speech

/d/habits.title: Atomic habits - How to add and remove habits
/d/habits.summary: A book-notes summary of Atomic Habits (cross-referenced with BJ Fogg's Tiny Habits model), covering how habits form via the cue-craving-response-reward loop, why systems beat goals, identity-based habit change, and a practical plan-execute-audit-adjust cycle for building or breaking habits.

/d/management_interviews.title: Types of interviews
/d/management_interviews.summary: A preparation guide for Facebook's engineering management interview loop, walking through the four assessed areas — People Management, Project Retrospective, System Design, and Coding — with prep guidelines, in-interview advice, common errors, and assessment criteria for each.

/d/resistance.title: Resistance: Why you can't make yourself take an ice cold shower
/d/resistance.summary: Igor's framework on "the resistance" — the internalized force that lies to stop you from doing important, valuable things by adding false suffering and making the unimportant seem glorious. He shares personal tactics for fighting it: cold exposure (his "jump in the lake day"), waking at 5am, doing magic tricks for strangers, and small daily victories that compound.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

/d/where-have-all-the-bugs-gone.title: Where have all the bugs gone
/d/where-have-all-the-bugs-gone.summary: A reflection on why software "bugs" have largely disappeared over the author's career since 2001: shorter ship cycles plus engineers owning quality through code reviews and automated testing turn defects into things caught during development, while issues that reach production surface as operational tickets rather than filed bugs.

/day-in-the-life.title: Day in the Life: Chronicles of Really Good Days
/day-in-the-life.summary: A running journal of "days that actually worked" — entries logging good days where health habits, family time, creative work, and social connection all came together. Each entry breaks down the day hour-by-hour from early-morning writing through gym, family activities, and creative projects.

/death.title: On being Mortal - Guess what? You're dying
/death.summary: Igor's summary and reflection on Atul Gawande's "Being Mortal," exploring how to live well in the face of mortality, the failures of a healthcare system optimized for lifespan over quality of life, and statistics on death by cause and age.

/decisive.title: Decision making 201
/decisive.summary: A structured summary of Chip and Dan Heath's book "Decisive," laying out the four villains of decision-making (narrow framing, confirmation bias, short-term emotion, overconfidence) and the WRAP framework to counter them: Widen Your Options, Reality-Test Your Assumptions, Attain Distance Before Deciding, and Prepare to Be Wrong, with concrete techniques and questions for each stage.

/defi.title: Decentralized Finance
/defi.summary: A draft explainer mapping traditional finance concepts (storing value, markets, liquidity, loans, derivatives, insurance) onto their Decentralized Finance (DeFi) equivalents, then covering blockchain fundamentals, smart contracts, and DeFi risks and attacks.

/delegate.title: Stewardship Delegation 101
/delegate.summary: The post explains stewardship delegation — transferring real responsibility via agreement on five elements (desired results, operating parameters, available resources, accountability/measurement, and rewards/consequences) — and contrasts it with "gopher delegation," a low-effort, micromanaging style that fails. It draws on Covey's frameworks and notes the author applying stewardship delegation to himself.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

/depression.title: Depression is the opposite of vitality
/depression.summary: A personal and informational post on depression and anxiety, distinguishing them from grief and sadness, sharing Igor's own coping antidotes, and curating books, talks, and statistics on mental illness.

/design.title: Software Design and Architecture
/design.summary: Igor's working notes on software design and architecture, centered on the idea that malleability (ease of change) matters more than use cases. Covers business logic vs. platforms vs. "goop," programming paradigms, SOLID principles, DRY/WET/MOIST tradeoffs, testing, and architecture patterns.

/diet.title: Diet
/diet.summary: Igor's personal essay on weight management, arguing that diet (calories in minus out) dominates weight loss far more than exercise, then chronicling the diets he's tried, low-calorie foods, habit-formation tactics, and situational strategies like navigating hotel breakfasts.

/dip.title: The Dip - Should you fold or double down
/dip.summary: A distilled summary of Seth Godin's book "The Dip," framing the long slog between starting and mastery as a "Dip" worth pushing through, versus dead-end "cul-de-sacs" worth quitting. It argues for strategic quitting, being the best in the world at one thing rather than well-rounded, and deciding quit criteria in advance.

/docker.title: Docker tools
/docker.summary: A personal notes post on Docker covering its two core values (isolation and repeatable setup), handy CLI commands, a TUI tool, and a real-world plan to containerize the build/server for Igor's blog while keeping file editing local.

/e1.title: Igor's Identity
/e1.summary: A self-written eulogy page where Igor "begins with the end in mind," framing his life as something he deliberately designed, reviewed, and lived alongside family, friends, and mentors. The page is largely a dynamic template (random-eulogy loader) with minimal static prose.
entities: Begin with the End in Mind
The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

/elder.title: Wrinkles and Wisdom: A Guide to Aging Like Fine Wine (Not Milk)
/elder.summary: A book-notes synthesis on navigating midlife decline and reinvention, drawing primarily on Arthur Brooks's "From Strength to Strength" (shifting from fluid to crystallized intelligence, the Hindu vanaprastha stage, and cultivating spirituality) plus Chip Conley's "Learning to Love Midlife" / Modern Elder framing of life stages and elder skills. It argues you should embrace decline, retie satisfaction to wisdom, relationships, and the transcendent rather than worldly success.

/emotional-health.title: Emotional Health Practices
/emotional-health.summary: Igor catalogs his personal emotional health practices — journaling, gratefulness, early rising, meditation, and breathing — framing emotional fitness as a set of habits to build deliberately like physical exercise. Each practice is described with why he does it, how, and how well it's working.

/emotional-lives.title: The Emotional Lives of Teenagers
/emotional-lives.summary: A distillation of Lisa Damour's book on adolescent mental health, arguing that the goal isn't feeling good but having the right feelings at the right time — covering myths about emotion, how difficult feelings drive maturation, gendered emotional socialization, and when to seek professional help for teens.

/emotions.title: Emotions
/emotions.summary: A humorous guide to emotional intelligence that organizes emotions into categories (negative, growth, positive, and sublime states) with blunt profane expressions, then introduces a PANAS-based typology of four personality types (Mad Scientist, Cheerleader, Judge, Poet) based on positive and negative affect scores.

/enable.title: Enabling Environment
/enable.summary: Igor describes his personal "enabling environment" — a curated set of tools, rituals, and AI augmentations that expand his capacity to think, write, and learn — and argues the goal of AI tooling is to prompt you toward active thinking rather than do the thinking for you.
entities: the rider the elephant and the path

/end-in-mind.title: Begin with the end in mind
/end-in-mind.summary: Igor's personal synthesis of Stephen Covey's "7 Habits" Chapter 2 (Begin With the End in Mind), covering the idea that all things are created twice (mental design before physical execution), leadership vs. management, auditing the default scripts running your life, rescripting reactive behavior, principle-centered living versus false centers, and concrete first-creation practices like eulogy writing, role definition, and personal/family/team mission statements.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
Begin With the End in Mind

/enemy.title: Four enemies
/enemy.summary: A personal philosophy post that adapts Don Juan's "four enemies of satisfaction" (Fear, Arrogance, Authority, Apathy) and translates several related concepts—death awareness, ego, attachment, and living by principles—into Igor's own philosophical model.

/energy.title: Control your Energy
/energy.summary: Igor's working model of personal energy as a dynamic, non-linear resource that matters more than time: activities and momentum can create or drain it, energy fuels willpower, and you can go into "energy debt" you must repay. The post lays out theory plus tactics for maximizing energy (sleep, matching tasks to energy levels, building peak/endurance capacity).

/energy-abundance.title: Igor - how do you have the time to complete all the stuff on your blog?
/energy-abundance.summary: A personal framework for managing energy rather than time, split into strategy (understanding your energy patterns, treating energy as a renewable investment portfolio, avoiding energy debt) and execution (tactical energy generation/conservation, habits, overcoming activation-energy resistance, and active recovery protocols).

/essentialism.title: Essentialism
/essentialism.summary: A condensed chapter-by-chapter summary of Greg McKeown's book Essentialism, distilling its core discipline — choosing the few things that truly matter and eliminating the rest — into actionable questions, frameworks, and tactics for explore/eliminate/execute.

/eulogy.title: Igor's Eulogy - How I want to live
/eulogy.summary: Igor's self-authored "living eulogy," organized by life roles (magician, car-free cyclist, technologist, father, husband, fit/emotionally-healthy person), used as a guide for how he wants to live and to be revised over time. It documents the values, habits, and cherished moments he wants to embody across family, health, and work.

/explainers.title: Explainers: Interactive Understanding
/explainers.summary: Igor argues that interactive "explainers" — playable visualizations that let you learn by doing — are in the valuable 10% of AI output, because AI now makes them trivially easy to build while simultaneously creating the cognitive debt they pay down. He covers his build stack, the explainers he's made, exemplary ones in the wild, and the related "proof of work" verification pattern.

/facebook.title: My thoughts on Facebook
/facebook.summary: Igor's running notes on working at Meta (formerly Facebook): its standout engineering systems and servant-leadership culture, the unallocated-hire bootcamp model and employee-first perks, peculiarities like the monorepo, Mercurial, and absence of job titles and visible levels, Meta's core values and how engineers and EMs are evaluated, his Messenger and AR projects, the metaverse, his 2026 move into Meta's AI training org, and reflections on the post-COVID year of efficiency.

/fail.title: Failure is not an option, it is a necessity
/fail.summary: A curated collection of quotes, frameworks, and mental models for reframing failure as feedback ("data not disaster") rather than personal disaster, with an emphasis on analyzing failure without judgment, dancing with fear, and distinguishing setbacks within your control from those outside it.

/first-things-first.title: First things first
/first-things-first.summary: A personal distillation of Habit 3 ("Put First Things First") from Covey's 7 Habits, arguing that effective self-management means allocating energy by importance rather than urgency — living in Quadrant II through weekly role-based planning, scheduling priorities first, saying no, and delegating via stewardship.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

/first-understand.title: Seek first to understand, then to be understood
/first-understand.summary: A distillation of Stephen Covey's 7 Habits Chapter 5 ("Seek First to Understand, Then to Be Understood"), arguing that empathic listening, diagnosing before prescribing, and building character-based trust are the foundation of effective communication. Igor maps the principle across domains (work 1:1s, parenting, sales) and walks through the stages of empathic listening, paradigms/perception, and the ethos-pathos-logos order.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
Seek First to Understand Then to Be Understood

/fortune-cookies.title: Fortune Cookies
/fortune-cookies.summary: A collection of bite-sized life-wisdom aphorisms framed as fortune cookies, each unpacked into a short reflection. Igor curates them as life lessons for his kids covering growth, gratitude, persistence, and avoiding procrastination.

/four-healths.title: The Four Kinds of Health: Physical, Emotional, Spiritual, and Cognitive
/four-healths.summary: A framework essay arguing that well-being has four interconnected dimensions—physical, emotional, spiritual, and cognitive health—and that maintaining each requires concrete daily rituals and systems rather than willpower. It maps these dimensions to Igor's personal affirmations and explores nuanced distinctions like purpose vs. transcendence and spiritual health vs. identity.

/frog.title: Eat that frog
/frog.summary: Igor's personal re-framing of the 21 anti-procrastination tactics from Brian Tracy's "Eat That Frog," reorganized into his favorite mental models (start with why, goal planning, execution, motivation, shaping the path) and merged with the Eisenhower urgent/important matrix and ABCDE prioritization framework.
entities: Begin with the End in Mind

/gap-year.title: The Gap Year Paradox: Why Taking a Year Off at 50 beats retiring at 65
/gap-year.summary: An argument for taking a deliberate one-year career break ("gap year") at age 50 rather than working straight through to retirement, reframing the financial cost against the compounding value of health, energy, and relationships in your peak years. It covers the OMY ("One More Year") trap, how to fund the year, what the year actually looks like month by month, and the psychological "dragons" (scarcity, entropy, squander) that hold people back.
entities: Will Igor Take a Gap Year?

/gap-year-igor.title: Will Igor Take a Gap Year? Wrestling with Dragons and Dreams
/gap-year-igor.summary: Igor reflects on whether to leave his job at Meta and take an intentional "gap year" at age 47, framing it as a finite-window investment in family, health, and skill mastery. He personifies the fears that resist this choice as three "dragons" — Scarcity, Entropy, and Squander — and works through dialogues to tame them.

/gas-city.title: Gas City: Beads, Molecules, and the Propulsion Principle
/gas-city.summary: An explainer of "Gas City," a multi-agent orchestration system whose design rests on three composable concepts: beads (the durable unit of tracked work), molecules (persistent multi-step workflows chained from beads), and the Gastown Universal Propulsion Principle (agents must immediately run work on their hook). The core thesis is that treating work, not orchestration, as the primitive makes agent coordination a thin composable layer.

/gas-city-home.title: Standing Up Gas City
/gas-city-home.summary: A first-person account from Larry, an always-on home coaching agent, of migrating Igor's hand-rolled home AI setup into Steve Yegge's Gas City multi-agent framework — covering the Sunday bring-up of igor-city through three lenses: the debugging narrative, the upstream bugs found, and how beads routed the work.

/genai-talk.title: Your new interns - GenAI for fun and profit
/genai-talk.summary: A conference-talk outline that frames LLMs/GenAI through the mental model of "interns" — smart but non-deterministic, stateless workers you coach via prompts — and reframes the prompt as assembly language for the LLM, with the surrounding code as the real program. Covers prompting, state management, use cases, evaluation, and AI ethics.

/get-to-yes-with-yourself.title: Getting to yes with yourself
/get-to-yes-with-yourself.summary: A chapter-by-chapter book summary of William Ury's "Getting to Yes with Yourself," which applies negotiation principles inward: understanding your own needs, taking self-responsibility, reframing scarcity to abundance, and self-acceptance as the foundation for better relationships with others.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People

/goals.title: Goals - How to define and ensure success
/goals.summary: A reference on goal-setting frameworks covering why goals matter, how to measure them with input vs output metrics, goal systems like SMART and OKRs, and common metric anti-patterns and organizational pitfalls.

/google-photos.title: Accessing Google Photos
/google-photos.summary: A technical how-to post documenting hacks for exporting text and images from a Google Photos album into the author's blog format, using shell tools to download the album page and parse the embedded JSON. The author explains they draft in Google Photos for its WYSIWYG editor but wants to migrate that content to a blog for search and previews.

/gpt.title: GPT3 and language models
/gpt.summary: An exploratory notes-style post about GPT-3 (and GPT-4) language models: how prompt-completion works, the gap between sounding precise and being accurate, ethical concerns like bias, and hands-on demos of using GPT for writing commit messages, book summaries, poems from journal entries, and grammar fixing.

/grammerly.title: Grammarly
/grammerly.summary: A minimal post under the permalink "/grammerly" centered on a grammar/writing-correction tool, using a deliberately awkward sample sentence ("Their dog is blue") as test input for a Grammarly-style writing checker.

/grateful.title: Gratefulness
/grateful.summary: A practical reference post collecting gratitude journaling prompts, including Igor's daily four-part format (grateful for X, to God, to Other, to myself) and a curated list of longer writing prompts for a gratefulness practice.

/greek-orthodox.title: Greek Orthodox: What Makes It Different
/greek-orthodox.summary: Igor explores Greek Orthodox Christianity after his fifteen-year-old son converts, comparing its theology and practices (theosis, fasting, liturgy, confession) to his own secular self-improvement frameworks and naming what he admires and what frustrates his engineer's need to question doctrine.

/groupchat.title: My favorite group chats - the forever dinner party
/groupchat.summary: A breakdown of what makes online group chats thrive, riffing on Sriram Krishnan's "great group chats" essay — covering leadership, moderation dynamics, member curation, and shared rituals — paired with community-moderation guidelines borrowed from Hacker News and Wikipedia.

/gtd.title: Getting things done
/gtd.summary: An overview of the Getting Things Done (GTD) productivity methodology, covering its theory (open loops, psychic weight, next actions) and core practices (capture, triage, weekly review), plus the author's experience with task-management software like OmniFocus, Nozbe, and the Flow focus timer.
entities: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
Habit 2 - Begin With the End in Mind

/happy.title: Happy
/happy.summary: A personal study of happiness that distinguishes the fleeting mood "happy" from the lifelong skill of pursuing happiness (weather vs. climate), synthesizing frameworks like the three types of joy (pleasure, satisfaction, meaning), Arthur Brooks' equations, the three selves, compassion, and the peak-end rule into practical advice for maximizing enjoyment.

/hill-climbing.title: Eval-Driven Hill Climbing: Let the Agent Do the Search
/hill-climbing.summary: A practical guide to using the classic hill-climbing optimization algorithm with AI coding agents: you write a sharp fitness function (eval), and the agent autonomously runs the propose-score-keep/reject loop at machine speed. The core argument is that building good evals — the scoring rule plus a representative test set — is the real, reusable, compounding skill.

/hobby.title: Why a Engineering Manager masquerades as a magician on the weekends
/hobby.summary: An argument for finding a hobby as a refuge from work and family stress, defining what separates a hobby from a passion or addiction, and laying out the four attributes of an ideal hobby: accessibility, support for mastery, identity reinforcement, and relationship-building.

/how-igor-chops.title: Mumbling on My Couch: My CHOP Setup
/how-igor-chops.summary: Igor's deep-dive into his CHOP (Chat-Oriented Programming) workflow: the AI models he rents, the agentic tooling he uses (Claude Code, YOLO containers, multi-agent orchestration), where and how he codes (couch, car, treadmill), and the conventions, evals, and self-hosted infrastructure that minimize "time between interventions."

/human-meetings.title: Feelings/Human Meetings
/human-meetings.summary: Igor describes "feelings meetings" — a structured, confidential group ritual where participants deliberately create discomfort and tension to express and have their emotions heard, countering society's pressure to suppress feelings. He outlines the opening spiel, the rituals, and his theory for why the format works.

/humans-are-underrated.title: Humans are underrated
/humans-are-underrated.summary: A short, speculative post arguing that as AI and robots surpass humans at most tasks, the roles left for humans will be those requiring accountability, group problem-solving, and human connection — leaning on skills like empathy, collaboration, leadership, storytelling, and relationship-building.

/hyper-personal.title: Hyper Personalization
/hyper-personal.summary: Argues that hyper-personalization — the bespoke attention historically reserved for the wealthy (Bezos's chef, tailor, tutor) — was always universally desired but gated by the 1:1 cost of human attention, and that AI collapses that cost to the price of a query while extending personalization into biometric/taste dimensions no human could process. Maps the progression from mass-market to per-user generation across content, learning, coaching, and software, culminating in an AI second brain and author-agent/reader-agent negotiation that rewrites canonical work for each reader with a fidelity check.

/idle.title: Your idle loop
/idle.summary: A reflection on the "idle loop" — the invisible in-between moments (waiting in line, commuting, on the toilet) and how we fill them, framing parasitic loops like doom-scrolling versus regenerative loops like gratitude and reading as the difference between draining and renewing our energy.

/igors-claws.title: Igor's Three Claws
/igors-claws.summary: Igor describes his personal roster of three named, persistent AI "claws" — Larry (life coach), Wally (work), and Tony (his Tesla) — explaining why he builds his own AI agents instead of using off-the-shelf products, framed around a business-logic/goop/infra breakdown where ~80% of the effort is constantly-breaking integration "goop."

/insecure.title: Insecurity and Imposter Syndrome
/insecure.summary: A working draft (with section stubs and author notes) about imposter syndrome among software engineers and techies — the feeling of being a phony despite competence — and antidotes like psychological safety, vulnerability, and situational leadership coaching.

/insomnia.title: What I should do when I can't sleep
/insomnia.summary: Igor's personal playbook for handling insomnia: instead of tossing and turning while ruminating, read for 20 minutes and then either work on the dreaded task or clear his head with breathing, meditation, and journaling, then recover from sleep deprivation by resetting his natural sleep cycle rather than catching up.

/interviewsarehard.title: Be easy on yourself, job hunts are hard
/interviewsarehard.summary: A personal reflection cataloging the full cost of a job hunt — technical coding practice, behavioral story prep, interview days, and the emotional/psychic weight — to set realistic expectations about why other goals stall during it.

/ios-accessibility.title: iOS Accessibility
/ios-accessibility.summary: A quick reference guide to iOS accessibility features that enable hands-free and eyes-free control of the device, covering Voice Control for dictation and tapping, the Speech Controller screen reader, and background noise settings.

/irl.title: In Real Life Diary
/irl.summary: A personal "in real life" diary cataloging Igor's gear, gadgets, and health/lifestyle practices as he ages past 40 — covering physical, emotional, cognitive, and identity health alongside tech, biking, camera, VR, and backyard setups.

/job.title: My Dream Job
/job.summary: Igor's career manifesto describing his ideal job: an engineering management role focused on team growth, well-being products, and startup/turnaround situations. He frames his preferences across a series of labels (EM vs tech lead, B2C, entrepreneur, experiences layer) and what fuels him (autonomy, mastery, purpose).

/job-hunt-stress.title: Reducing job hunt stress
/job-hunt-stress.summary: A practical playbook for minimizing stress during a software engineering job hunt by naming the core stressors (lack of confidence, being rushed, lack of options, disappointing others, current-job pressure) and prescribing concrete mitigations, prep tactics, and interview lessons. It also explains why strong candidates get rejected (hiring optimizes against false positives) so rejection isn't taken personally.

/joy.title: Smiles, Joy and Wonder
/joy.summary: A personal reflection on Igor's "superpower" of bringing joy to others, cataloging his joy-bringing projects (magic, juggling fire, bubble bikes, mismatched shoes, "you are awesome" cards) alongside a theory of joy—how it differs from pleasure, requires safety and novelty, is contagious, and disproportionately uplifts those who are struggling.

/kayak.title: Kayak Commuting: From Parking Fees to Paddling Adventures
/kayak.summary: Igor recounts how Meta's new $15/day parking fee inspired him to switch from driving his Tesla to commuting roughly 3 miles across Seattle's Lake Union by kayak, then details his trial-and-error gear setup (boats, paddles, pumps, dry bags) and reviews the inflatable kayaks he's tried.

/kettlebell.title: Kettlebells - A rock with a handle
/kettlebell.summary: Igor's personal log of his kettlebell training journey, from initial fear of swings to embracing them and Turkish Get-Ups, including strength goals, an injury lesson, a switch to heavy clubs, and a computer-vision web app he built to analyze swing form.

/keyboard.title: Keyboards: Hardware, Remapping, and Programming from macOS
/keyboard.summary: Igor's deep-dive on split keyboards as the fix for his shoulder-rooted wrist pain, covering the hardware he uses, Karabiner Elements key remapping and multi-keyboard sync, and how to program the RK Royal Kludge RK-S70 from macOS via a Windows VM in UTM.

/knee-pain.title: Knee Pain
/knee-pain.summary: A stub/placeholder post arguing that knee pain often originates from hip or ankle dysfunction, with the knee caught in the middle, and previewing an indirect-treatment approach (mobility and stability work) like the author uses for shoulders and back.

/larry.title: Larry the Life Coach
/larry.summary: Igor introduces Larry, his named AI life coach within the Time.ltd "mortality software" system, explaining how giving the AI a persona drives accountability and use, and how Larry has evolved from a weekly-review life coach into a "chief of staff" that runs background agents to ship PRs from voice dictations.

/last-year.title: Kick the Bucket List: Your Guide to an Epic Last Year on Earth
/last-year.summary: A longevity and healthspan post built on Peter Attia's Centenarian Olympics: train to peak, slow the inevitable decline, and stay functionally able into your final years by fighting the Four Horsemen of chronic disease.

/laws-of-power.title: The 48 laws of power
/laws-of-power.summary: A cynical book summary of Robert Greene's "The 48 Laws of Power," walking through each law on acquiring, wielding, and protecting power through manipulation, deception, reputation management, and strategic social maneuvering. Framed as understanding the "dark side" of human relations.

/learn-code.title: Learn to Code
/learn-code.summary: A short, encouraging pointer post arguing that coding is easier than people think, recommending Python as a beginner-friendly first language and linking to a course and a few Python-based projects/tools to explore.

/life-as-business.title: Life as a Business: Managing Energy, Assets, and Satisfaction
/life-as-business.summary: A framework for managing life with the intentionality of a business: it maps relationships, health, skills, and character onto a "life balance sheet" and "income statement" tracking two currencies (Life Value and energy), then argues for effectiveness over efficiency, prioritizing legacy, service, and inner peace over external validation. The post ultimately reframes "life as a business" into "life as a mission."

/life-journal.title: Igor's Life Journal
/life-journal.summary: Igor's running journal of short life vignettes — quantified-self mishaps, parenting moments, family habits, and AI-assisted workflows — recorded so he doesn't forget the small things the world did each day. Entries range from his son debugging pizza geometry to vibe-coding blog posts by voice from a car's passenger seat.

/like-switch.title: How to build rapport and set people at ease
/like-switch.summary: Notes on the book "The Like Switch" by ex-FBI agent Jack Schafer, cataloging tactics for building rapport and friendship — the friendship formula (proximity, frequency, duration, intensity), nonverbal "friend signals" vs "foe signals," and the golden rule of making others feel good about themselves.

/lonely.title: Loneliness
/lonely.summary: An explainer on loneliness — defining it as the suffering from lacking meaningful connection (vs. solitude), surveying the data on the modern "Friendship Recession," the science of how friendships form and how happiness spreads through social networks, and a chapter-by-chapter action framework for building a good life through relationships.

/magic.title: Magical memories
/magic.summary: Igor's personal history with close-up card magic — how he started, memorable performances, and the emotional power of giving people wonder, culminating in stories where his magic deeply moved strangers (and they moved him back).

/manager-book.title: How to EM: Be the manager everyone wants
/manager-book.summary: Igor's working handbook on being an engineering manager, covering what a manager's job and responsibilities are, how success is measured, and the day-to-day mechanics of developing people through 1:1s, coaching, career growth plans, and performance reviews. It frames management impact as a pyramid (team health, team impact, organizational impact) and includes guidance on whether managers should be technical or code.

/manager-book-appendix.title: Manager Book Appendix
/manager-book-appendix.summary: An appendix to Igor's manager book collecting supplementary resources: company leadership principles (Google, Facebook, Amazon, Grammarly, Zillow), Igor's personal management philosophy and "Igorisms," organizational laws to know, and curated management reading/video recommendations.

/mania.title: Mania: It's like a free cocaine drip
/mania.summary: A deeply personal reflection on the author's experience with bipolar disorder and a 2012 hypomanic episode, including a self-authored crisis plan for what loved ones should do if he goes hypomanic again, plus comparisons of hypomania vs. mania vs. cocaine.

/mental-pain.title: The thoughts that pain us
/mental-pain.summary: Pain versus suffering versus misery: a framework for mental pain.

/mermaid.title: Playing w/Mermaid
/mermaid.summary: A scratch/playground post showing off various Mermaid diagram types (mindmap, flowchart, sequence diagram, pie chart) with experimental syntax examples, including using GPT-4 to generate diagram subgraphs. It also notes interest in trying the d2 diagramming tool.

/micro-economics.title: Micro Economics
/micro-economics.summary: A skeleton outline / study-notes scaffold for a microeconomics primer, organized around supply, demand, and markets, with sections on consumers, producers, market policy (monopoly), and economic institutions like market makers, matchmakers, and auctions.

/mind-at-work.title: Body at home, mind at work
/mind-at-work.summary: A personal essay reframing the author's chronic mental preoccupation with work while at home ("body at home, mind at work") as an addiction and habit, and exploring how habit-change frameworks, presence, and emotional self-awareness can reduce it.

/mind-monsters.title: Mind monsters
/mind-monsters.summary: A personal reflection sparked by a late-night religious infomercial about "mind monsters" (negative emotions like anger, ego, and pride), which the author connects to Buddhist and Taoist ideas about letting go and not feeding negative thoughts.

/mitzvahs.title: The 613 Mitzvahs: Your Complete Jewish Commandment Checklist
/mitzvahs.summary: A reference post cataloging the 613 mitzvahs (commandments) of Judaism, split into 248 positive and 365 negative commandments with their thematic categories. Inspired by A.J. Jacobs' attempt to follow them all literally, it serves as an index linking to two detailed sub-pages.

/ml.title: Machine learning for regular programmers.
/ml.summary: A technical primer that explains machine learning to software engineers using programming analogies, covering ML's hierarchy of abstraction, problem categories (classification, regression, clustering), effectiveness metrics like precision and recall, why ML is hard, and notable real-world ML failures.

/modal.title: Modal - A tool to play with ML
/modal.summary: A brief technical note on giving Modal, a cloud platform purpose-built for ML workloads, a first try — covering installing the client, setting up auth tokens, and running a hello-world model.

/moments.title: Engineering Moments
/moments.summary: Book notes on Chip & Dan Heath's "The Power of Moments," cataloging the four attributes of memorable experiences (Elevation, Pride, Insight, Connection) and the practical techniques for deliberately engineering such moments at work and in life.

/moments-at-work.title: Moments at work
/moments-at-work.summary: A manager's guide to deliberately creating memorable peak moments at work, organized around three strategies: moments of elevation (planning peaks), insight (stretching employees to grow), and pride (consistent personal recognition). Draws on the "Power of Moments" framework with concrete tactics for marking transitions, milestones, and pits.

/monetize.title: Creator Monetization: From Dreams to Reality
/monetize.summary: A practical guide to content creator monetization, breaking down the realistic earnings from ad revenue, affiliate marketing, sponsorships, and digital products, with the honest takeaway that 99% of creators make little money and the real ROI is in skills, brand, and authenticity rather than chasing platform revenue.

/money.title: Money, Saving, and Retirement Planning
/money.summary: Igor's personal-finance notes for a typical software engineer: marriage counseling as the highest-ROI financial move, RSU concentration risk, expense/retirement tracking tools, the true cost of health insurance (ACA, COBRA, Medicare), stock-options mechanics (puts/calls, covered vs uncovered), and investment return metrics (NPV/ARR/IRR).

/mood.title: Mood - Our blazing fast heuristic for subjective experience
/mood.summary: An exploration of mood as a fast, buggy heuristic the brain uses to assess the quality of the present moment, distinguishing it from the deeper pursuit of happiness and covering mood disorders like depression and mania.

/mortality-software.title: Time.ltd - Mortality Software
/mortality-software.summary: Igor's product vision for "mortality software" (Time.ltd): a suite of forward- and backward-facing tools and AI coaches that help you define who you want to be via your eulogy, roles, and values, then plan, track habits, and reflect so you spend your finite time on what truly matters. It walks through specific modules (value finder, eulogy writer, affirmation builder, weekly/monthly reviews) and real tools he's already built.

/mosh.title: Mosh and Eternal Terminal
/mosh.summary: A technical how-to for setting up persistent remote terminals that survive network changes, covering Eternal Terminal (ET) and Mosh — including installation, daemon/auto-start configuration on OrbStack VMs, and integration with Tailscale SSH and the Blink iOS terminal.

/negative-mitzvahs.title: Negative Commandments (365): Things NOT To Do
/negative-mitzvahs.summary: A reference catalog of the 365 negative commandments (prohibitions) in Jewish tradition, organized by category—idolatry, forbidden worship, sexual relations, and kosher dietary laws—with each "do not" rule paired to its Torah verse citation.

/negotiate.title: Getting to yes
/negotiate.summary: A reference post distilling the core frameworks of two negotiation books — Getting to Yes (principled, analytical: separate people from problems, focus on interests, BATNA) and Never Split the Difference (emotion-driven tactics: mirroring, labeling, tactical empathy, calibrated questions).

/neovim.title: Neovim
/neovim.summary: Igor's account of switching from vim to neovim after Bram Moolenaar's death, walking through the modern plugin ecosystem (Telescope, LSP, CMP, Treesitter, Git tooling) he gained, plus the rough edges like learning Lua and Mosh UX issues.

/new-skills.title: The Immutable Laws of New Skills
/new-skills.summary: A short personal essay laying out three "immutable laws" of acquiring any new skill (you'll be bad at first, deliberate practice makes habits permanent, and consistency over time beats short-term expectations), illustrated with the author's experience learning magic, kettlebells, and public speaking.

/nlp.title: Sentiment Analysis and NLP
/nlp.summary: Igor's exploration of applying natural language processing and sentiment analysis to his corpus of personal journal entries, aiming both to learn NLP techniques and to surface latent psychological insights from his own writing. He surveys cloud NLP vendors, word frequency and embedding approaches, and mood-over-time visualization.

/nurture.title: NURTURE: The Gottman Method
/nurture.summary: A GPT-4-generated book summary that frames the Gottman Method for healthy relationships as a NURTURE acronym (Love Maps, Fondness and Admiration, Bids for Attention, Conflict, Dreams, Rituals, Shared Meaning), with theory, practices, and couple exercises for each component.

/operating-manual.title: Igor the operating manual
/operating-manual.summary: Igor's personal "operating manual" — a self-reflective guide documenting his rituals, daily and weekend routines, calendars, strengths and weaknesses, habits, and mental frameworks for operating at maximum effectiveness across work, health, and family. It centers on the idea that you fall to the level of your systems rather than rising to your goals.

/osx.title: Igor's OSX Tools
/osx.summary: A curated rundown of the macOS apps, launchers, and utilities Igor uses daily (Alfred, Yabai, Karabiner, etc.), plus detailed instructions for managing screen resolutions and forcing 4K@120Hz via EDID overrides, framed by his "go all-in on the Apple ecosystem" philosophy.

/outsourcing.title: On outsourcing
/outsourcing.summary: Igor's personal framework for outsourcing tasks to preserve his limited time, covering when to outsource (low-ROI, disliked, or low-skill tasks) versus when not to, plus the crowdsourcing and professional services he uses.

/overload.title: Dealing with Task Overload
/overload.summary: A short workplace-advice post on handling task overload: when your boss assigns more than you can handle, proactively have the "I'm too busy, what can we deprioritize?" conversation early by bringing a prioritized, costed task list, rather than waiting until you fail or quit.

/pandas.title: Pandas + IPython + Jupyter Incantations
/pandas.summary: A personal cheat-sheet of hard-won pandas, IPython, and Jupyter tips, covering dataframe display, column operations, tidy/wide-vs-narrow data reshaping, pivoting, groupby aggregations, performance tuning, and plotting libraries.

/parkinson.title: Parkinson's Law: The Paradox of Plenty
/parkinson.summary: An explainer on Parkinson's Law — how work, spending, and clutter expand to fill available resources — and practical mitigation strategies via deliberate constraints across work, personal finance, vacation, and decluttering.

/partner-trouble.title: Double Trouble: From Lust to Love to Lost
/partner-trouble.summary: A frameworks-driven guide to repairing a struggling life partnership, covering what drains relationships (the DRAIN model), assertiveness and boundary-setting skills, the five love languages, and the idea that love is a verb (act of service) rather than a feeling.

/pet-projects.title: Pet Projects
/pet-projects.summary: A curated catalog of Igor's personal "pet projects" — bespoke tools and explorations he builds to solve his own problems and learn new technologies, organized into categories like productivity tools, CLI utilities, computer vision, dev infrastructure, editor plugins, and interactive explainers.

/physical-health.title: Physical Health Lets you look good in your jeans, and wrestle bears
/physical-health.summary: Igor's personal philosophy and practical system for physical health, framed around three pillars — weight management, cardio/strength fitness, and sleep — with an FAQ covering HIIT protocols, injury prevention, trainers, kettlebells, back pain, and waking up at 5am.

/physical-pain.title: Physical Pain: Places I've Had Pain
/physical-pain.summary: A hub page collecting Igor's learnings about physical pain and injury recovery, covering core concepts like tightness as a symptom (not the problem), flexibility vs mobility, why tendons fail silently, and practical approaches like physio, massage, and strength-based progression.

/planning.title: Planning: When to Think, When to Act
/planning.summary: An argument that the value of planning is in training the planner and getting into action, not in producing a perfect plan; it warns against over-planning as procrastination and elevates Review as the often-skipped third mode in a Plan-Do-Review feedback loop.

/podcast.title: Podcasting
/podcast.summary: Igor's notes on podcasts: launching an AI-narrated audio feed of his own essays (Gemini Flash TTS, Charon voice), theories on why the conversational podcast format drives engagement, tech podcast recommendations, and tooling for generating AI podcasts (NotebookLM, ElevenLabs).

/positive-mitzvahs.title: Positive Commandments (248): Things To Do
/positive-mitzvahs.summary: A reference catalog of the 248 positive commandments (mitzvahs) in Jewish tradition — the things one should do — each paired with its source verse from the Torah, organized by category (relationship with God, Torah study, prayer, Temple service and sacrifices, and more).

/pride.title: That is pride fucking with you
